---
title: "3.3.1 Primary memory"
subtitle: "RAM, DRAM and SRAM · Hardware P120-P122"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        .reveal .clean-table th:first-child, .reveal .clean-table td:first-child { width: 19%; white-space: nowrap; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## 3.3.1 Primary memory

### Random access memory (RAM)

All computer systems come with some form of <span class="term">RAM</span> (随机存储器).

The word <span class="mark">random</span> refers to the fact that <span class="mark">any memory location in RAM can be accessed independent of which memory location was last used</span>.

When you run an application or program, data is retrieved from <span class="term">secondary storage</span> and placed temporarily into RAM. Access time to locate data is much faster in RAM than in secondary or off-line devices.

## RAM Features

### What the textbook emphasises

Features of RAM include:

::: {.tight-list}
- it has <span class="mark">read/write capability</span>; it can be written to or read from
- the data can be changed by the user or the computer
- it is used to store data, files, part of an application or part of the operating system currently in use
- it is <span class="term">volatile memory</span> (易失性内存), which means memory contents are lost when powering off the computer
:::

In general, the <span class="mark">larger the size of RAM</span> the faster the computer will operate.

## RAM When It Becomes Full

### Why more RAM helps

RAM never runs out of memory; it continues to operate but just becomes <span class="mark">slower and slower</span> as more data is stored.

As RAM becomes full, the CPU has to continually access secondary data storage devices to overwrite old data on RAM with new data.

By increasing the RAM size, the number of times this has to be done is considerably reduced; thus making the computer operate more quickly.

## Figure 3.59

### Primary memory

![Figure 3.59 Primary memory placeholder](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-1-primary-memory-ram-dram-sram-p120-p122/Figure 3.59 Primary memory placeholder.svg){fig-align="center" width="88%"}

<span class="figure-note">Figure 3.59 screenshot placeholder: Primary memory.</span>

## DRAM

### Transistors and capacitors

There are currently two types of RAM technology:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">dynamic RAM (DRAM)</span>
- <span class="term">static RAM (SRAM)</span>
:::

Each DRAM chip consists of <span class="mark">transistors</span> and <span class="mark">capacitors</span>.

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">capacitor</span> — this holds the bits of information (0 or 1)
- <span class="term">transistor</span> — this acts like a switch; it allows the chip control circuitry to read the capacitor or change the capacitor's value
- DRAM <span class="mark">must be constantly refreshed</span>
- the capacitor needs to be re-charged every <span class="mark">15 microseconds</span>
:::

## Figure 3.60

### DRAM

![Figure 3.60 DRAM placeholder](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-1-primary-memory-ram-dram-sram-p120-p122/Figure 3.60 DRAM placeholder.svg){fig-align="center" width="88%"}

<span class="figure-note">Figure 3.60 screenshot placeholder: DRAM.</span>

## DRAM Advantages

### Compared with SRAM

DRAMs have a number of advantages over SRAMs:

::: {.tight-list}
- they are much less expensive to manufacture than SRAM
- they consume <span class="mark">less power</span> than SRAM
- they have a <span class="mark">higher memory capacity</span> than SRAM
:::

## SRAM

### Flip-flops and speed

A major difference between SRAM and DRAM is that <span class="term">SRAM</span> (静态随机存储器) <span class="mark">doesn't need to be constantly refreshed</span>.

It makes use of <span class="term">flip-flops</span> (触发器), which hold each bit of memory.

SRAM is much faster than DRAM when it comes to data access:

::: {.tight-list}
- SRAM access time is typically <span class="mark">25 nanoseconds</span>
- DRAM access time is typically <span class="mark">60 nanoseconds</span>
:::

## Figure 3.61

### SRAM

![Figure 3.61 SRAM placeholder](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-1-primary-memory-ram-dram-sram-p120-p122/Figure 3.61 SRAM placeholder.svg){fig-align="center" width="88%"}

<span class="figure-note">Figure 3.61 screenshot placeholder: SRAM.</span>

## Table 3.9

### Differences between DRAM and SRAM

::: {.clean-table}
| DRAM | SRAM |
|---|---|
| consists of a number of transistors and capacitors | uses flip flops to hold each bit of memory |
| needs to be constantly refreshed | does not need to be constantly refreshed |
| less expensive to manufacture than SRAM | has a faster data access time than DRAM |
| has a higher memory capacity than SRAM | CPU memory cache makes use of SRAM |
| main memory is constructed from DRAM |  |
| consumes less power than SRAM |  |
:::

## Memory Cache

### Why SRAM is used there

DRAM is the most common type of RAM used in computers, but where absolute speed is essential, for example, in the CPU's <span class="term">memory cache</span> (高速缓存), SRAM is the preferred technology.

Memory cache is a high-speed portion of the memory. It is effective because most programs access the same data or instructions many times.

By keeping as much of this information as possible in SRAM, the computer avoids having to access the slower DRAM.

## Classroom Check

### Keep the RAM distinctions precise

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- that RAM is <span class="mark">volatile</span> and can be <span class="mark">written to and read from</span>
- that any memory location in RAM can be accessed <span class="mark">independently</span> of the last one used
- that DRAM uses <span class="mark">transistors and capacitors</span> and needs <span class="mark">constant refresh</span>
- that SRAM uses <span class="mark">flip-flops</span> and does <span class="mark">not</span> need refresh
- that SRAM is <span class="mark">faster</span> and is used in <span class="term">memory cache</span>
- that DRAM is <span class="mark">cheaper</span> and has <span class="mark">higher capacity</span>
:::

## Bridge

### Next: ROM

The next deck stays within <span class="term">3.3.1 Primary memory</span> and covers <span class="term">ROM</span>.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
